filter_index.video_title: Why We Love The Buran Ghati Trek | Indiahikes

snow_or_rain_notes: June: snow at the last alpine campsite of Dhunda; vast snowfields from Dhunda till the pass; ~150 m rappel section; snow trekking for about an hour after the pass
Latter part of June: snow melting; snow close to pass may not be enough for big slides
Autumn: snow in smaller patches; frost at Dhunda campsite; chances of snowfall at Buran Ghati Pass when weather goes bad; chances of first winter snow by October
temperature_notes: Summer night: drops to zero at higher campsite
Summer layers: 4 warm layers in May; 3 warm layers towards June
Autumn night: drops to zero to -4 to -5 °C at higher campsite

stated_difficulty: Taxing due to steep ascent at over 13,000 ft and boulder navigation
terrain_challenges: Boulder field just after the pass crossing (snow-covered in May, exposed June onwards)
Small exposed section overlooking a river near the River campsite
Steep climb to the pass beyond Dhunda
altitude_or_weather_risks: Trek starts at over 9,000 ft, higher than usual
Altitude gain of 5,585 ft (9,415 ft to 15,000 ft) over seven days
Risk of Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) above 10,000 ft for any trekker
Fitness does not eliminate AMS risk if ascent is too fast
High-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers carried by Indiahikes team

fitness_benchmark: Walk, jog or run 5 km in under 32 minutes (minimum recommended fitness level)
approval_requirements: Fitness approval from the Indiahikes team is required 20 days before the trek date; without it you will not be allowed on the trek
At least 3 runs from the last 1 month must meet the fitness criteria of the trek
Fitness record may be rejected if you fail to link your fitness profile or do not meet the required fitness level at least three times in the past month
prep_window: 6 to 8 weeks before the trek begins
A 6-week plan is provided to get trek-fit if you are not currently active

experience_themes: Summer: Buran Pass is a dramatic wall of ice in June; rappelling down an ice wall while crossing the pass
Summer: Vast snowfields from Dhunda till the pass; ~150 m rappel from the top of the pass; trekking in snow for around an hour after crossing the pass; multiple snow slides
Summer: Snow starts melting in latter part of June; snow close to the pass may not be enough for big slides
Summer: Trail changes from white to greens and yellows; wildflowers bloom on meadows and around campsites; grasslands turn bright green with bright yellow flowers
Autumn: Fiery, glorious colours on the trail; shades of green near base camp and through forests/grasslands; yellows, oranges, and browns closer to the pass
Autumn: Snow in smaller patches; ice on Buran Ghati wall has melted, exposing rocky terrain; trek down rocky patch while descending; no rappelling; trek is a bit easier
primary_watchouts: Latter part of June: snow melting; snow close to pass may not be enough for big slides
Autumn: no snow and no rappelling down the ice wall of Buran Pass in October
Autumn: chances of snowfall at Buran Ghati Pass when weather goes bad
Autumn: chances of witnessing the first winter snow of the year by October
Trekkers specifically wanting to rappel down the Buran Pass ice wall in October (autumn)
Trekkers wanting big snow slides in the latter part of June (snow melting)
best_for: Adventure seekers wanting to rappel down an ice wall at Buran Pass (summer, June)
Trekkers wanting snowfields, snow slides, and snow trekking (summer)
Trekkers wanting wildflowers and colourful meadows (summer, May to June)
Trekkers wanting fiery autumn colours and a slightly easier trek (autumn)
not_ideal_for: Trekkers specifically wanting to rappel down the Buran Pass ice wall in October (autumn)
Trekkers wanting big snow slides in the latter part of June (snow melting)
Trekkers who cannot prepare well or lack stamina
Trekkers who climb too high too soon regardless of fitness
Trekkers who ignore pre-existing medical conditions or safety protocols
